content:
What I Learned About System Design (Part 7)

4 min read
Just now

--

What sharding actually is
Database sharding is the process of taking one large database and splitting it into smaller ones.
Picture a college’s student records in which every student, every course, every grade, all sitting in a single database. As that database grows, even a simple lookup (“find student #48,213”) means searching through a huge amount of data, and that gets expensive. Sharding is the fix: split that one large database into several smaller ones, so any given piece of work only has to touch a fraction of the total data.
There are two ways to split it: vertical sharding, which splits by columns, and horizontal sharding, which splits by rows.
Vertical sharding
Vertical sharding makes sense when some columns are used constantly and others are barely touched at all.
Take a users table: id, username, emailthese are accessed on basically every request, login included. Now add bio, profile_picture, last_login_history — heavier, and touched far less often, maybe only when someone actually visits a full profile page.
Vertical sharding means splitting these onto separate databases not just separate tables in the same database (that’s normalization, a different and more basic technique), but genuinely separate database servers:
Database A (fast, hit constantly): id, username, email
Database B (heavier, hit rarely): id, bio, profile_picture, last_login_historyDatabase A can stay small and fast, maybe even mostly cached in memory. Database B can sit on cheaper, slower storage, since it’s not fighting for attention on every single request. That’s the actual point — physically separating load, not just tidying up a schema.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The real cost of this split: you can no longer use a normal foreign key or a JOIN across the two, since those only work within a single database. You still keep a shared id in both places, but it's just a plain value now — nothing enforces that it still matches on both sides. If you need both the username and the bio, your application has to query both databases separately and stitch the result together itself. And if a delete to Database A succeeds but the matching delete to Database B fails, you can end up with an orphaned row nobody's tracking. Handling that usually means one of a few things: compensating logic that retries the second write (the same idea as the Saga pattern from Part 5), soft deletes so orphaned data is simply never shown, or a periodic reconciliation job that sweeps up mismatches on a schedule. None of it is free, a single database gives you these guarantees automatically; sharding trades that away for the ability to spread load across machines.
Horizontal sharding
Horizontal sharding makes sense when the workload itself needs to be spread across rows, same table shape, same columns, just too many rows for one database to comfortably hold or serve.
Say you’re sharding that same students table by row instead of by column. Shard A holds one chunk of students, Shard B holds another, and so on. The real question becomes: which student goes on which shard? There are three common strategies.
Range-based — split by ranges of a key, like student_id 1–100,000 on Shard A, 100,001–200,000 on Shard B. Simple, and great for range queries ("give me students 50,000 to 60,000" hits exactly one shard). The catch: if IDs are assigned in increasing order, all your newest, most active students pile onto whichever shard holds the highest range which would become a hotspot, right back to the "one server doing all the work" problem sharding was meant to solve. This gets mitigated by ranging on something naturally distributed instead of a raw sequential ID something like department or region, for instance, or by systems that automatically detect a hot shard and split it further once it crosses a load threshold.
Key-based (hash-based) — this is the same idea as consistent hashing from Part 3, just applied to rows instead of servers. Hash the student_id, use that hash to pick a shard. Load spreads evenly, no hotspots. The tradeoff: range queries no longer hit one shard — after hashing, a range of IDs is scattered randomly across all of them, so a range query now has to check every shard. This gets softened by keeping a separate index just for range/search queries, by hashing a composite key (like region + ID) so naturally related rows land near each other.
Directory-based (lookup table) — instead of computing the shard with a formula, you keep an explicit table mapping student_id → shard. Maximum flexibility, any row can move to any shard for any reason. The tradeoff: that lookup table itself becomes a new bottleneck and single point of failure, since every request has to check it first. This gets handled the way most single points of failure do, cache the mapping aggressively (since it rarely changes), replicate the lookup service across multiple instances, or let the application cache large chunks of the mapping locally and refresh only occasionally.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The theme across all three fixes, worth naming honestly: none of them make the underlying tradeoff disappear. They just move the cost somewhere more tolerable, extra indexing infrastructure, slower rare queries, or added caching and replication. That’s been the pattern through most of this series: distributed systems rarely solve a problem outright, they trade it for a smaller, more manageable version of itself.
That’s it for this part. If I’ve gotten something wrong or oversimplified somewhere, I’d genuinely love to hear about it in the comments. This is a learning in public thing for me, not a “here’s the definitive answer” thing.
